Inheriting Eco · Bologna 2026 Semiotics

Sharing an
Encyclopedia

A Thought Experiment
Andrea Valle  ·  Università di Torino
Centro internazionale di Studi umanistici "Umberto Eco"
Università di Bologna
01

The Code

Eco 1975

The code as bijection

Each element of Expression is put in relation with one element of Content. Adequate for ant chemotaxis, highway codes, formal grammars.

Holland · adaptive agents

Embodying the code

  • Code = rules governing detector / effector relations (sign functions)
  • Content resolves into action — sign production
  • Detectors and effectors are types: matching and instantiation
Hjelmslev · two readings

Expression / Content: two interpretations in tension.

  • Substantial — phonology vs. semantics; the Saussurean inheritance
  • Formal — trigger vs. response; input type / output type
  • The formal reading enables the law of total E/C reversibility
02

From Code
to Encyclopedia

Linear topology

The chain

A content token output by one agent becomes an expression token for the next. Expression and content are positions assumed by forms of material substances.

Eco 1984 · Model Q

The encyclopedia

A strongly connected graph linking units in a non-univocal manner. The dictionary is a degenerate case — an encyclopedia with simplified topology.

Perimetral conditions · liminal realism

Two problems at the boundary of semiosis:

  • How does it start? — The prime mover must lie outside semiosis. The subject is thrown into a world.
  • How does it stop? — Semiosis costs energy and time. Not "who speaks?" but who dies?
03

Signal Soup

Gedankenexperiment

Taking semiotic formalization seriously

Can these models be implemented and studied?

  • Eco practised the Gedankenexperiment from the Edenic language essay (1967) onward
  • Signal Soup follows this method — formalized enough to simulate
  • Can a shared encyclopedic structure emerge from communication rather than precede it?
The model

Environment & Agents

  • Coordination through stigmergy — indirect, no direct feedback
  • Polylogical, not dialogical — no dyadic pairs
  • Signals: 3-letter strings → 17,576 possible types
  • Each agent deals with a signal soup created by the other agents
Agent algorithm

Sign functions sf : d → e

  • Match — fire effector, replace signal in Environment, increment r
  • No match — link two random signals into a new sf (r = 1)
  • Memory cap sfmax = 10 — drop lowest-r sf if exceeded
The Band

A population sharing an Environment

  • Each Agent initialized with one random sf — r = 0, easily replaced
  • Environment seeded with one signal per Agent — size = n
  • Asynchronous — one randomly selected Agent per turn
  • A small, tightly integrated communicative circuit
04

What Emerges

t = 0
t = 2,000
Fig. 2 · single agent topology

Complexification

  • Loops — agent responds to its own output
  • Synonymy — different detectors, same effector
  • Chains — longer auto-response sequences
fig. 3 Agents' matching relations in Environment — 20-agent Band after 2,000 turns. Environment redundancy increases over time.
fig. 4 · emergent constants
≈ 5.6
sign functions / agent
1/3
type / token ratio

Not programmed. Robust across all band sizes and parameter variations.

t = 0
t = 2,000
fig. 5 Band topology · red edges = sign functions shared by 2+ agents · ~26% shared · majority idiosyncratic
05

Conclusions

Conclusion 1

The encyclopedic topology is not prior to communication — it emerges from it.

  • Each agent's graph crystallizes through interaction with Environment
  • Environment is constituted by other agents' outputs
  • The encyclopedia is endogenous to the communicative process
Conclusion 2

Sharedness operationalizes Eco's locality of the encyclopedia.

  • Each agent holds a local encyclopedia — partial, idiosyncratic
  • Sharedness = degree of overlap between local encyclopedias
  • Full alignment (the dictionary) is the degenerate case: zero idiosyncrasy
grazie · thank you
